draft.introduction: Hesiod’s Theogony narrates the birth of the gods from primordial Chaos through the succession of divine generations to Zeus’s establishment of cosmic order. It is the foundational text of Greek cosmogony.

translation.text: First of all there came to be Chaos, and then broad-bosomed Earth, the ever-sure foundation of all the deathless ones who hold the peaks of snowy Olympus, and dim Tartarus in the depth of the wide-pathed Earth, and Eros, fairest among the deathless gods.
translation.original_text: ἦ τοι μὲν πρώτιστα Χάος γένετ’ αὐτὰρ ἔπειτα Γαῖ’ εὐρύστερνος

translator_notes.note: Chaos (Χάος) does not mean “disorder” in Hesiod but “gap” or “yawning void.” Eros as a primordial force—older than the Olympian gods—places desire at the foundation of existence. This cosmogony influenced Plato, the Stoics, and the Neoplatonists.
